hypothesis: Prices that deviate significantly from their recent mean (beyond 2 standard deviations) tend to revert in liquid, range-bound ETFs where underlying fundamentals haven't structurally shifted. Liquidity-driven dislocations and short-term overreaction create exploitable mean-reversion opportunities in highly liquid ETF instruments.
universe: Liquid broad-market and sector ETFs: SPY, QQQ, IWM, GLD, TLT, and 11 SPDR sector ETFs (XLK, XLF, XLE, XLV, XLI, XLP, XLY, XLU, XLB, XLRE, XLC). High liquidity required for tight spreads.
entry_logic: Compute 20-day simple moving average and 20-day rolling standard deviation for each ETF. Buy when price crosses below lower band (SMA - 2*std). Short when price crosses above upper band (SMA + 2*std). Enter at next open after signal.
exit_logic: Exit long when price returns to the SMA (middle band). Exit short when price returns to SMA. Time-based failsafe: exit any position after 10 trading days regardless of mean reversion status. Do not hold through expiry or major macro events.
position_sizing: Position size inversely proportional to distance from the band at entry — larger positions for more extreme deviations. Maximum 15% of portfolio in any single ETF. Total gross exposure capped at 100% (long-only or small short allocation).
risk_management: Hard stop at 3 standard deviations from the band (i.e., if the price continues moving away by another full standard deviation beyond entry). Maximum holding period: 10 days. No adding to losers. Avoid entries within 5 days of FOMC meetings or CPI releases for bond/rate-sensitive ETFs.
known_failure_modes: Trending markets: ETF prices can stay beyond bands for extended periods during persistent trends (e.g., 2022 bond market selloff), causing large losses.
Macro regime shifts: fundamental repricing events (rate shock, earnings estimate revisions) make mean reversion invalid — the price is discovering a new equilibrium, not reverting.
Volatility expansion: when VIX spikes, bands widen but price can continue well beyond even the 3-std stop.
Works better on a basket of ETFs (diversified mean reversion) than on individual ETFs due to idiosyncratic regime risks.

historical_performance.notes: Significantly better applied to a basket of 10+ liquid ETFs versus any single ETF. The 20-day window balances signal quality vs. responsiveness. Performance degrades in 2022 (strong trending regime). Entry std of 2.0 is the sweet spot — 1.5 generates too many false signals, 2.5 reduces trade frequency excessively.

references: John Bollinger - Bollinger on Bollinger Bands (2001)
